title: Chapter 19: The Boardroom Part II
content: The emergency meeting summons arrives at 6:47 AM.

I know the exact time because I haven't slept. The glitching saw to that — all night, the ceiling of my apartment flickering between exposed beams and water-stained plaster, the sound of the Liffey bleeding through my radiator's clank. I've been living in two cities at once for six hours, and when my phone buzzes with Julian's name on the screen, I'm already dressed.

Sterling Capital at dawn is a different creature. The lobby is empty, the security desk manned by someone I don't recognize. The elevators hum with a lower frequency when they're not carrying hundreds of bodies. The air conditioning is already running — cold, sterile, recycled air that tastes like nothing. Like a hospital. Like a place where they've decided what temperature humans should exist at and never asked the humans.
The thirty-sixth floor is not empty.

Every senior person is here. The long mahogany table that usually seats twelve has been pushed wider, extra chairs brought in. Monitors are up. The Meridian acquisition — the deal that was supposed to close next week, the deal that was going to be Sterling's biggest move of the fiscal year — is on fire. And the man who lit the match is sitting at the far end of the table, hands folded, face placid.

Harvey Langdon. Vice President, Strategic Partnerships. Twenty-two years at Sterling Capital. A jaw like a parking barrier and the kind of smile that made you feel like you'd just been approved for something you hadn't asked for.

Julian stands at the head of the table. No, not stands. Commands. He's still wearing his coat — cashmere, charcoal — like he didn't bother taking it off. Like the meeting couldn't wait the ten seconds it would take to hang it up. His sleeves are rolled to the forearms. There's a fleck of something on his collar — toothpaste, maybe. A crack in the armor so small that no one else would catch it.
I catch it.

I take a seat along the wall. The junior position. The place where you sit when you're there to observe and not to speak. The cold air hits the back of my neck and I suppress a shiver that has nothing to do with temperature.

"Let's start with what we know," Julian says, and his voice is different. Crisis Julian. I've seen glimpses of him before — the controlled intensity, the way his consonants get sharper, like someone's taken a whetstone to his syllables. But this is the full expression. He's terrifying. Not because he's loud. Because he's so precise he could cut you with a clause.

He lays out the situation in four sentences. Meridian's bid strategy leaked. The competitor revised their offer within forty-eight hours. The deal is now in jeopardy. And the information traveled through a single channel.

He doesn't say Harvey's name. He doesn't have to. The silence in the room is so complete I can hear the air cycling through the vents — a low, sterile whoosh, like the building is breathing for us because we've forgotten how.

Harvey leans forward. He's had time to prepare. I can see it in the way his hands are positioned — loose, open, the practiced posture of a man who's been in more boardroom fights than I've been alive. He smells like sour coffee and something underneath — stale, like an anxiety that's been marinating for days.

"I know how this looks," Harvey says. "And I understand the impulse to look for a scapegoat. But the truth is simpler and more uncomfortable." He pauses. Lets the silence do its work. "Meridian's cybersecurity was compromised six weeks ago. We all saw the report. The leak came from their side, not ours."

It's smooth. God, it's smooth. He's not arguing — he's redirecting. Not defensive, but paternal. The wise elder correcting the children who've gotten ahead of themselves. I watch him lay it out: the timeline of the cyber breach, the known vulnerabilities in Meridian's system, the unfortunate coincidence that the competitor's revised bid landed so close to Sterling's strategy. Coincidence. He uses the word twice. Nobody uses the word "coincidence" twice unless they need you to believe it.

I glance at Julian. His jaw is tight — that faint muscular tension below his ear that I've mapped without meaning to, across three timelines, across a thousand moments of stress and control and the crackling space between what he says and what he means. He knows. I can see it in the set of his shoulders, in the way his fingers press against the table's edge just barely, in the stillness of his chest that says he's holding his breath in a way that looks like breathing.
He knows Harvey is lying.
But he doesn't have proof.

The meeting grinds on. Other voices — the CFO, the General Counsel, a woman from Compliance whose name I should remember but the glitching is making the edges of everything soft. The Irish Sea roars somewhere beneath the hum of the air conditioning, salt and cold and a thousand miles away and right here, right now, crashing against the cliffs of Clonakilty in a timeline I'm not supposed to be hearing.

Julian's not going to get what he needs from this meeting. Harvey's story is airtight, at least on the surface. The cybersecurity report is real. The vulnerability existed. The timeline technically works. It's a beautiful lie — beautiful because it uses true things to construct a false shape.
I know something about that. Three timelines' worth of true things constructing a shape that nobody else can see.

My laptop is open. The Osprey research is still loaded from yesterday — a completely different project, a different client, a different species of corporate unpleasantness. Osprey is government contracts, not acquisitions. Nothing to do with Meridian.
But espionage is espionage.

The patterns don't change. I learned that from the Osprey deep dive — the way information moves through off-channel communications, the timing of encrypted messages against public disclosures, the metadata fingerprints that people leave when they think they're invisible. Corporate, government, it doesn't matter. The architecture of stealing is universal.
My fingers are moving before I make the decision.

I pull Harvey's communication logs — the ones from the Meridian discovery request, the ones that are part of the record. I overlay them against the Osprey pattern matrix. I match the frequencies. The burst intervals. The routing through personal devices that don't fall under Sterling's IT policy because they were registered before the policy changed.
The correlation is devastating.

It's not proof. I need to be clear about that. It's not a smoking gun or a confession or a paper trail with Harvey's name on a check. What it is — what it actually, undeniably is — is a map. A topographical chart of coincidence so precise, so perfectly aligned with every known espionage signature, that it proves only one thing: the proof exists, and it hasn't been found yet.
I stand.
My chair scrapes against the floor. The sound is enormous in the tension-thick air. Every head turns. I feel the weight of their attention like a physical thing — cold and heavy and appraising.
"Actually," I say, and my voice sounds steadier than I am. "I have additional data."
Julian's eyes find me. He doesn't tell me to sit down. He doesn't say anything. He just watches, and the crack in his composure widens by a millimeter — not worry, not dismissal. Curiosity.

I walk to the front of the room. The cold air catches in my throat. I plug my laptop into the display system and the monitors flicker — for one terrifying second, I see a double image, the Clonakilty coastline bleeding through the corporate presentation like a ghost. Then it solidifies. The correlation map. Harvey's communication bursts, blue. The Osprey pattern matrix, red. The overlap, purple. So much purple.

"Corporate espionage uses consistent patterns regardless of sector," I say. "I've been mapping these signatures for the Osprey project. When I overlay Harvey's communication logs against the established pattern matrix—" I tap the screen. "—this is what we get."

I walk them through it. The timing of off-channel messages, precisely twelve to eighteen hours before each Meridian development became public. The routing through personal devices. The bursts clustered around dates that correspond to known competitor advisory meetings. Each point is circumstantial. All of them together form a shape so clear it's almost architectural.

"It's not proof of transmission," I say, meeting Harvey's eyes directly. "But it is proof of opportunity, means, and pattern. The proof this map shows exists somewhere. It just hasn't been recovered yet."
The room goes silent.

Not the productive silence of consideration. The held-breath silence of people watching a building collapse in slow motion. I can taste it — the electric absence of sound, the sterile air, the collective realization that the ground has shifted and no one has figured out where it's settled yet.
Harvey moves first.

He stands. His chair doesn't scrape — he's too controlled for that. He places both hands flat on the table and leans forward, and when he speaks, his voice is warm and measured and designed to make me feel like a child who's wandered into a conversation between adults.
"Who is this?" He doesn't look at me when he says it. He looks at Julian. "Some junior analyst manufacturing connections to impress her boss?"

The room temperature drops another three degrees. I feel it against my skin, the sterile cold of a place that has decided what I'm worth. Several people shift in their seats. The woman from Compliance looks down at her notes.

Three months ago, this would have worked. Three months ago, I would have felt the floor tilt, the walls close in, the familiar vertigo of being small in a room built for big people. Three months ago, Harvey Langdon's dismissive glance would have sent me back to my seat with my laptop closed and my pulse in my throat.

But three months ago, I hadn't stood in a Dublin warehouse while a man with a gun decided whether I was worth the bullet. I hadn't sat across from a Parisian socialite who could ruin lives with a lunch reservation. I hadn't watched the fabric of reality come apart at the seams and held myself together through nothing but stubbornness and a phone app that shouldn't exist.
I don't flinch.

"The connections are real," I say. My voice is level. Not loud — level. The kind of calm that comes from having survived worse than a boardroom. "The question isn't whether I'm manufacturing them — it's whether you are."
The room inhales.

It's the only way to describe it — a collective intake of breath, sharp and involuntary, like a body hit with cold water. I hear it. I feel it. The pressure in the room changes as fourteen senior executives process what I just said. A first-year analyst just accused a Vice President of manufacturing cover stories in front of the entire leadership team.

Harvey's face does something fascinating. A micro-expression — there and gone in a fraction of a second — that I recognize from a hundred Julian-reading sessions. The flicker of genuine rage beneath the polished surface. He covers it fast, but not fast enough.
Julian's eyes meet mine across the table.
And in them — in those Arctic grays that I've watched shift like weather across three timelines — I see something I've never seen before.
Pride.

Not the professional respect he showed me in his office. Not the careful acknowledgment of competent work. This is raw and unguarded and utterly out of his control. He looks at me like I've just done something he didn't know was possible. Like I've surprised him, and Julian Vance is not a man who gets surprised.

He turns back to the table. "The investigation continues," he says. His voice is steel. "Harvey, you'll make your devices available for forensic review. All of them." Harvey starts to object and Julian cuts through him like a blade through paper. "All of them. Today."

The meeting dissolves. Faster than it should — people fleeing the scene, escaping the oxygen-depleted room. The air conditioning hums its sterile indifference. I gather my laptop with fingers that are trembling now, now that it's over, now that the adrenaline is draining and everything it was holding back is rushing in.
I make it to the hallway.

The hallway is empty. Gray carpet, glass walls, the morning light coming through the eastern windows at an angle that makes everything look like it's been washed in thin milk. I lean against the wall. The Irish Sea is so loud — waves crashing against rocks, the cry of gulls, the howling wind through the gap in Julian C's cottage door. I close my eyes and the world tilts.
I sway.

My hand finds the wall. The carpet is solid beneath my heels but my body doesn't believe it. I'm in two places, three places, the membrane between timelines wearing thin as old cloth. The INTEGRITY number pulses behind my eyes. Forty-three percent. Almost half gone. Almost half of whatever holds me together, dissolved.

Footsteps. Not the sharp clip of someone walking with purpose — a measured stride, unhurried. The scent reaches me before he does. Cedar and sandalwood, warm and dark and anchored. Julian's cologne, cutting through the recycled air like a blade of something alive.

He's standing in the middle of the hallway. Not cornering me against the wall like before — that was a power move, calculated, controlled. This is different. He's in the open. In the light from the floor-to-ceiling windows. Both hands at his sides. His coat is gone now, sleeves still rolled, and the morning light catches the gold in his watch, the gray in his eyes, the softness of an expression I've only seen once before — in his office, when he caught me mid-glitch, when he said my name like it mattered.
"That," he says, "was the single most impressive thing I've ever seen a first-year do."
I try to form a response. The words pile up behind my teeth and don't arrange themselves into sentences.

"Harvey's been skating for a decade," Julian continues. "A full decade. Knows where every body is buried because he helped bury half of them. Nobody's ever put a dent in his armor." Something shifts in his face — not a smile, not quite. The ghost of one. "You just put a crack in the ice."

I should be glowing. I should be riding the high of intellectual checkmate, the rush of proving myself in a room that expected me to be furniture. And there's some of that — a bright coal of satisfaction burning somewhere beneath my ribs.
But it's buried under exhaustion so deep it feels structural.

The glitching is constant now. The Irish Sea roars through the air conditioning. I can smell salt — actual salt, cutting through the cedar and sandalwood, cutting through the sterile nothing of corporate air. I can feel the damp of Clonakilty on my skin. The gap between timelines isn't a gap anymore; it's a gauze curtain, and I'm standing in the folds.
I sway again. Harder this time.
His hand catches my elbow.

Warm. Firm. His thumb presses against the inside of my arm where the pulse runs close to the surface, and I can feel him taking inventory — the rapid beat of my heart, the tremor in my muscles, the fine sweat at my hairline that has nothing to do with the air conditioning.
"Avery."

My name. The second time he's said it. But it sounds nothing like the first. In his office, it was gentle — a door held open, an invitation. Now it's urgent. It's the voice of a man watching something happen that he doesn't understand and can't control and isn't willing to accept.
"What's happening to you?"

I look up at him. The morning light makes his edges softer, his jaw less severe, the lines around his eyes more visible. He's scared. Julian Vance, who stared down a crisis like gravity, who cut through a room with consonants sharpened on a whetstone — he's scared. For me.
And I almost say it.

The truth. The whole impossible, sprawling, multi-timeline, reality-unraveling, app-on-my-phone truth. About the Fork. About Paris and Clonakilty and the other versions of him — the one who held me while I shook, the one who made me laugh, the one whose cottage smells like peat and sea air. About the INTEGRITY counter ticking down like a bomb. About how I'm dissolving, piece by piece, across three lives I can't hold and three men I can't choose between.
I almost say all of it.
Instead, I say: "I need to show you something."
I pull my phone from my pocket. My hands are shaking — his grip on my elbow tightens, steadying me. I unlock the screen. The Fork app icon glows at me, blue-white and impossible. I tap it.
INTEGRITY: 43%

The three paths bloom across the screen. Timeline A: Wall Street, the glass tower, the man in front of me. Timeline B: Paris, the gallery, the one who painted. Timeline C: Clonakilty, the coast, the one who stayed. Three dots on a map. Three versions of the same love story, branching like arteries from a single heart.
I turn the screen toward him.

Julian looks down at it. The light from the three paths catches in his gray eyes — blue, gold, green, refracting like he's staring into something that shouldn't exist. His grip on my elbow doesn't loosen. His expression doesn't change. He's reading. Processing. The way he reads a market, a contract, a lie — parsing the impossible shape of what I'm showing him.

The morning light holds us both in the hallway, suspended between the cold boardroom behind us and whatever comes next. Three timelines glow on a screen. One man stares at a map of lives he doesn't know he's living. One woman waits for the moment when everything changes.
It's about to.
It's about to change everything.
